---
# Edit first: update title, authors, and affiliations.
title: "How Energy Price Shocks Reach Small Manufacturers"
author:
  - name: "Sofia Novak"
    email: "sofia.novak@example.org"
    corresponding: true
    affiliation:
      - "1"
affiliations:
  - id: "1"
    name: "Applied Policy Lab"
# Replace abstract with your project summary before sharing.
abstract: |
  This policy brief summarizes how higher energy costs affect smaller producers
  and which policy tools can ease short-run adjustment. The example keeps the
  prose short and decision-oriented while preserving the metadata needed for a
  clean typstR brief.
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  typstR-typst: default
typstR:
  format-variant: brief
  keywords:
    - energy prices
    - small firms
    - industrial policy
  report-number: "PB 001"
  funding: |
    This research was supported by the Industrial Transition Observatory.
---

## Key Findings

<!-- Replace this starter narrative with your project-specific text. -->
- Energy-cost spikes reduce margins fastest for smaller manufacturers with thin
  cash buffers.
- Short-term liquidity support works best when paired with clear efficiency
  upgrade incentives.
- Targeted communication reduces delays in firm take-up of emergency schemes.

## Background

Provide the policy context in one or two short paragraphs. Focus on why the
issue matters now, which firms are most exposed, and what decision makers need
to know first.

## Evidence

Summarize the most relevant statistics, comparisons, or survey findings that
support the brief's message. Keep the evidence section readable for a policy
audience rather than a specialist academic one.

## Policy Implications

Translate the evidence into a short set of practical recommendations, including
which measure can be implemented quickly and which one addresses longer-run
competitiveness.

## References
